concepts.title: Types of Isomerism
concepts.conceptBody:
Isomerism

Two or more substances having the same molecular formula but different structural or spatial arrangements are called isomers. They are of two types:

concepts.title: Sturctual isomersm
concepts.conceptBody:
Structural isomerism:

This type of isomerism due to the difference in structures of coordination compounds.

(i) Ionization isomerism: Complexes that have the same empirical formula and are produced by the interchange of the position of the ligands inside the complex zone and outside the complex zone are called ionisation isomers. They give different ions on ionization. For example

$\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}+\text{NO}_{2}^{-}$

$\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}}\text{Cl}\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right) \right]\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}}\text{Cl}\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right) \right]}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

(ii) Coordination isomerism: 

Ionic coordination compounds that contain complex cations and anions can exist as isomers if the ligands associated with the two metal atoms are exchanged. For example:

(1) $\left. \text{Pt}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}} \right]\left[ \text{PtC}{{\text{l}}_{6}} \right]$ and $\left[ \text{PtC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}} \right]\left[ \text{PtC}{{\text{l}}_{4}} \right]$

(2) $\left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}} \right]\left[ \text{Cr}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]$ and $\left[ \text{Cr}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}} \right]\left[ \text{Co}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]$

(iii) Linkage isomerism: Isomerism which results when a given ligand is joined to the central atom through different atoms of the ligand. For example

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{5}}\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right) \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \\ \text{and }\quad & \left[ \text{Co}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{5}}(\text{ONO}) \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Here, the ligand $\text{NO}_{2}^{-}$may attach with the central ion either through O-atom or through $\text{N}$-atom.

(iv) Hydrate isomerism: This type of isomerism arises due to the different position of water molecules inside and outside the coordination sphere. For example

$\left[ \text{Cr}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{6}} \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ and $\left[ \text{Cr}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{5}}\text{Cl} \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Violet colour Blue green colour

(v) Ligand isomerism: Some ligands themselves are capable of existing as isomers, for example diaminopropane can exist both as i.e.

 

when these ligands are associated to form complexes, these complexes are isomers of each other.

e.g. ${{\left[ \text{Co}{{(1,2\text{-diaminopropane })}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}$and

[Co (1, 3-diaminopropane ${{\left. _{2}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}$

concepts.title: Stereoisomersim
concepts.conceptBody:
Stereo isomerism

Stereo isomerism in coordination compounds arises due to different spatial arrangement of ligands around the central metal ion.

concepts.title: Geometrical isomersim for coordination no 4
concepts.conceptBody:
Geometrical isomerism

In complexes ligands may occupy different sites. When the ligands in question occupy adjacent positions it is called ‘Cis’ (Cis, Latin, meaning “On this side”) and when they occupy opposite sites, it is called ‘trans’ (trans, Latin meaning “across”). Therefore, geometrical isomerism is also called “Cis - Trans “isomerism. This type of isomerism is not possible for complexes of CN 2 or 3 or for CN 4, tetrahedral complexes.

In this complexes all ligand positions are adjacent not opposite. However, cis-trans isomerism is common in square planar and octahedral complexes.

(a) Square planar

(b) Octahedral

(1) $\text{CN}-4\text{Sq}$ planar complexes

The following types of four coordinated sq. planar complexes show cis-trans isomerism.

(i) $\mathbf{M}{{\mathbf{A}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

$\left[ \text{Pt}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]\quad $ diammine dichloro $\text{Pt}(\text{II})$

$(\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$and $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ ligands are adjacent) ($\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ ligands are opposite )

Cis and trans isomers may be distinguished by dipole moment measurement

(ii) $\text{MAB}{{\text{X}}_{2}}$

$\left[ \text{Pt}\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)\text{PyC}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]\left( \text{Py}= \right.$ pyridine $\left. {{\text{C}}_{5}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}~\text{N} \right)$

(iii) $[\text{MABCD}]$

${{\left[ \text{PtN}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH} \right)\text{PyN}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}$Ammine hydroxylamine pyridine nitro $\text{Pt}$ (II)

In such complexes three of the ligands $(\text{B},\text{C}$ or $\text{D})$ may be trans to $\text{A}$.

Such complexes are best denoted as $[M<AB>CD]$, the ligands in the angular brackets are trans to each other.

(v) Geometrical isomerism is also found in square planar complexes containing unsymmetrical bidentate ligands e.g. $\left[ \text{M}{{(\text{AB})}_{2}} \right]$, gly $=\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}$

concepts.title: Geometrical isomersim for coordination no 6
concepts.conceptBody:
(2) $\text{CN}-6$ (Octahedral complexes)

Geometrical isomerism in octahedral complexes is also very common. The familiar examples include compounds of the type:

(i) $\left[ \text{M}{{\text{A}}_{4}}{{\text{X}}_{2}} \right]$

(ii) $\left[ \text{M}{{\text{A}}_{4}}\text{XY} \right]$

(iii) $\left[ \text{M}{{(\text{AA})}_{2}}{{\text{X}}_{2}} \right]$

(iv) $\left[ \text{M}{{(\text{AA})}_{2}}\text{XY} \right]$

(v) $\left[ \text{M}{{\text{A}}_{2}}{{\text{X}}_{2}}{{\text{Y}}_{2}} \right]$

Where $\text{M}=\text{Co}$ (III), Cr (III), Rh (III), Pt (IV) etc.

$\text{A}-\text{A}=$ Symmetrical bidentate ligands

$\text{X}\,\,\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\,\,\text{Y}=-$ ive ligands.

For example

(i) ${{\left[ \text{Cr}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{4}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}$

(ii) ${{\left[ \text{Co}{{(\text{en})}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right]}^{+}}$

A few complexes of the type $\left[ \text{M}{{\text{A}}_{3}}{{\text{X}}_{3}} \right]$ are also known e.g. $\left[ \text{Rh}{{(\text{Py})}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{3}} \right]$

Largest numbers of geometrical isomers are found for complex of the type [MABCDEF] where each ligand is different e.g. [Pt $(\text{Py})\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)\text{ClBrI}$.

Unsymmetrical bidentate ligands also produce cis-trans isomers, e.g. [Cr(gly)$\left. _{3} \right]$.
